//: HS onboarding arc (docs/design/hs_onboarding_events.md, Sonnet 5, Stage 2 of the Act-1 fix plan). Closes Epic 5 ('Narrative Event Generator / Tutorial', docs/game-idas/HIGH_SCHOOL.md), previously a one-line stub, and the actual bug it exposed: a fresh age-16 freshman had zero first-day content and instead fired rookie_season_events.json's pro-clubhouse cascade (see that file's rewritten header for the other half of this fix). 11 events, all scope:'avatar' -- a week-one chain (days ~2-9, a beat a day: hs_first_day -> hs_meet_coach -> hs_tryouts -> hs_first_practice -> hs_first_game_nerves, with hs_lunchroom running in parallel off hs_started) plus a semester spine (hs_first_report_card/hs_report_card_slipping at ~debut+21, hs_coach_checkin recurring, hs_crosstown_rival_seed at ~debut+14, hs_homecoming at ~made_team+45). EVERY event carries { "field": "tier", "op": "==", "value": 0 } -- not just the entry -- so the arc self-gates to the HS tier the moment an avatar graduates, and a gen-2 heir's own tier keeps the redundant guard honest with zero reasoning about flag lifetimes (the rookie batch's own 'gate all, not just entry' call, mirrored). Events 7/8 partition on the new gpa field (>=2.5 solid vs <2.5 slipping) and share flag_inactive got_report_card, so exactly one fires per avatar per HS career -- the OR-is-two-events idiom. hs_coach_checkin alone carries no one-shot flag (a genuine recurring pulse, cooldown-paced). Contacts: coach_hs (Coach Malone, the HS varsity coach -- deliberately distinct from coach_reyes, the pro clubhouse coach) and hs_friend (Marcus, a classmate) are new registry entries; events 1 and 7 reuse the existing family_mom thread. hs_crosstown_rival_seed's 'opponent' relationship target is best-effort: at HS tier the opponent pool may be empty on a given roll, and per the existing empty-pool skip precedent the relationship consequence is then a no-op while set_flag hs_rival_seeded still lands, so the beat always completes regardless. No new ConsequenceKind/RelationshipTargetSelector/schema change anywhere in this batch -- pure content + the two new SubjectFields (Tier, Gpa) shipped alongside it. Day-2 competition audit (design doc §5): the only other event that can hold on day 2 of a fresh HS save is hs_dating_events.json's hs_crush_forms at weight 0.05, loaded first by alphabetical file order -- an acceptable ~5%/day slip that time-shifts (never drops) a week-one beat, left as-is per the design doc's recommendation. PHONE-SPLIT PASS (2026-07-10, Sonnet 5, docs/progress.md's two 'Events vs Messages' entries): every one of the 33 choices now carries an authored 'outcome' (the Events feed's immediate resolution line). Event-level fire-time 'text_message' ships on 7 of the 11 events (hs_first_day/hs_meet_coach/hs_lunchroom/hs_first_report_card/hs_report_card_slipping/hs_crosstown_rival_seed/hs_homecoming); hs_first_practice/hs_first_game_nerves/hs_coach_checkin deliberately ship none (universal beats or a recurring pulse -- spam risk). hs_tryouts is the one exception to 'event-level, fire-time': its verdict is authored as a CHOICE-level delayed text instead ({ body, delay_days: 1 } on all 3 choices, identical body) -- Coach Malone's roster call lands the morning after tryouts regardless of which choice was picked, not at the moment of the tryout itself.

events.prompt: First bell of freshman year. New building, a locker that won't open, a schedule you're pretty sure is wrong. Mom texts: 'Knock 'em dead today, kiddo.'

choices.label: Walk in like you own the place
choices.outcome: You push through the doors like you belong there. A few upperclassmen clock the swagger. Nobody stops you.

choices.label: Head down, find your classes
choices.outcome: You keep your head down and your schedule folded in your fist. By third period you've stopped getting lost.

choices.label: Ask her to pick you up early
choices.outcome: You text Mom before first bell. She says yes without asking why — which somehow makes it worse.

events.prompt: Coach Malone stops you in the hall. 'You're the freshman who can supposedly hit. Tryouts are Thursday. Don't make me regret knowing your name.'

choices.label: Promise you'll be there early
choices.outcome: "Early's good," Malone says, already walking away. "Early's what I like."

choices.label: Play it humble
choices.outcome: You shrug and say you'll try your best. Malone almost smiles. Almost.

choices.label: Tell him he won't regret it
choices.outcome: Malone raises an eyebrow. "We'll see, freshman." He's still watching you when you walk away.

events.prompt: Tryouts. Forty kids, fifteen spots, a coach with a clipboard who hasn't smiled once. Your name gets called for the first round.

choices.label: Leave it all on the field
choices.outcome: You sprint like the roster's already posted. Legs burning, lungs on fire — you don't let up once.

choices.label: Play smart, don't force it
choices.outcome: You pace yourself, hit your spots, let the tape speak for you instead of your lungs.

choices.label: Hype up the other freshmen to settle your nerves
choices.outcome: You crack jokes between drills, and the other freshmen loosen up. Even Malone's clipboard cracks a smile — maybe.

events.prompt: You made it. First official practice -- Coach Malone runs the team into the ground before anyone touches a ball. Around the tenth sprint, a few guys glance over to see if you'll break.

choices.label: Set the pace, don't break
choices.outcome: You take the lead on sprint ten and don't look back. A couple guys stop glancing over — now they're just trying to keep up.

choices.label: Just survive it
choices.outcome: You grit through it, one sprint at a time, and you're still standing when the whistle finally blows.

choices.label: Crack a joke to loosen everyone up
choices.outcome: You crack wise mid-sprint and half the team nearly trips laughing. Coach is not amused. Everyone else is.

events.prompt: First game tomorrow. You're in the lineup. Tonight, staring at the ceiling, every version plays out -- the ones where you're a hero and the ones where you're not.

choices.label: Visualize the good version until you fall asleep
choices.outcome: You run the good version on a loop until your eyes finally close on the part where you don't strike out.

choices.label: Get up and take fifty swings in the garage
choices.outcome: You flip on the garage light at midnight and swing until your hands ache. It helps more than sleep would have.

choices.label: Text a teammate who's just as nervous
choices.outcome: You text a teammate who's just as wound up. Trading bad jokes back and forth beats staring at the ceiling alone.

events.prompt: Lunch, day three. Tray in hand, nowhere obvious to sit. A table of kids from your grade clocks you standing there -- one waves you over before it gets awkward.

choices.label: Take the seat
choices.outcome: You sit. Introductions happen fast — first names, a joke about the cafeteria pizza, and just like that you have a table.

choices.label: Sit with the team instead
choices.outcome: You cross to the team's table instead. Nobody questions it — you're one of them now, cafeteria included.

choices.label: Eat alone and scout the room
choices.outcome: You eat alone at the end of a table, cataloguing who sits where. Efficient. Lonely. You'll take the intel.

events.prompt: First report card of high school hits the mailbox. Mom reads it twice and sticks it on the fridge before you're even home. 'We are SO proud of you.'
events.text_message: Putting this on the fridge. So proud of you, kiddo.

choices.label: Let yourself enjoy it
choices.outcome: You let yourself feel it — really feel it — grades taped to the fridge, your name in the school paper's honor roll blurb.

choices.label: Set your sights higher
choices.outcome: You fold the report card in half and start planning next quarter before this one's even sunk in.

choices.label: Downplay it, keep grinding
choices.outcome: You wave it off in the hallway when someone mentions it. Grades are grades. There's a game Friday.

events.prompt: Coach Malone drops a printout on the bench next to you -- your grades. 'No pass, no play. You want to keep that uniform, you fix this. I'm not asking.'

choices.label: Commit to turning it around
choices.outcome: You tell him straight: it gets fixed. Malone nods once, like he's heard promises before and is choosing to believe this one.

choices.label: Ask a classmate for tutoring
choices.outcome: You corner a classmate after class and ask, flat out, for help. It's not cool. It works.

choices.label: Argue grades don't matter if you can play
choices.outcome: You push back — stats over report cards. Malone's printout stays on the bench between you, unmoved.

events.prompt: Coach Malone catches you after the bell. 'How you holding up, kid? Season's long. School, ball, home -- a lot to carry at your age.'

choices.label: Tell him the truth
choices.outcome: You let it out — school, ball, home, all of it. Malone just listens, which somehow helps more than advice would.

choices.label: Say you're fine, keep moving
choices.outcome: You say you're fine and keep moving toward the field. Malone lets it go, but he's watching a little closer now.

choices.label: Ask him for extra reps
choices.outcome: You ask for more work instead of less. Malone raises an eyebrow, then blows the whistle for one more round.

events.prompt: Your buddy shoves a phone in your face -- a clip of the shortstop from Westbrook, the crosstown school, running his mouth about your team. 'He said your name, man. By name.'
events.text_message: He's still talking. You seeing this?

choices.label: Let your game do the talking
choices.outcome: You don't reply to the clip. You just circle the date on the schedule where you play Westbrook next.

choices.label: Fire back online
choices.outcome: You fire back in the comments before you can think better of it. It gets forty likes in ten minutes. Also, everyone's talking about it now.

choices.label: Laugh it off, screenshot it for later
choices.outcome: You laugh it off in front of your buddy — and quietly screenshot the clip for the locker room wall.

events.prompt: Homecoming's this weekend. Half the school's going, tickets are twenty bucks, and your crew keeps asking if you're in. First real 'everyone's there' night of the year.

choices.label: Buy a ticket, go all in
choices.outcome: You buy the ticket and go all in — bad music, gym decorations, the whole overpriced night. Worth it.

choices.label: Go, keep it low-key
choices.outcome: You show up, say hi to everyone once, and slip out before the slow songs start.

choices.label: Skip it, rest for the game
choices.outcome: You skip it and get a full night's sleep instead. Somewhere across town, a gym is loud without you.
